title_en: The Seerah — The Madinan Period and Major Battles
content_en:
Ibn Kathir's account of the Madinan period of the Prophet's ﷺ mission is among the most detailed and hadith-grounded sections of al-Bidayah wan-Nihayah, drawing on the vast body of Companion narrations preserved in the major hadith collections to reconstruct the key events, decisions, and spiritual dimensions of the decade between the Hijra (622 CE) and the Prophet's ﷺ death (632 CE).

The arrival in Medina and the Prophet's ﷺ immediate establishment of the mosque (the Masjid al-Nabawi) as the center of the new community's social, religious, and political life are described in detail. Ibn Kathir emphasizes the participatory nature of the mosque's construction — the Prophet ﷺ himself carried bricks and mud alongside his Companions, reciting: 'O Allah, the real good is the good of the Hereafter; forgive the Ansar and the Muhajirin.' The Constitution of Medina (Sahifat al-Madinah) is referenced as a foundational document establishing the multi-tribal political framework of the new Islamic polity.

The three major battles — Badr (2 AH), Uhud (3 AH), and Khandaq (5 AH) — receive extended treatment. Ibn Kathir's approach to the Battle of Badr is notably attentive to the theological dimension: the Quranic revelation that accompanied and followed the battle ('You did not kill them, but Allah killed them' — Quran 8:17) is integrated into the historical account rather than treated as external commentary. The theological point — that divine agency operates through human action without displacing it — is central to the Islamic philosophy of history that Ibn Kathir implicitly presents throughout.

The account of Uhud reflects the Quranic meditation on the trial: the initial Muslim success, the disobedience of the archers who left their position, the Qurayshi cavalry counterattack, the wounding of the Prophet ﷺ, and the Quranic response that transformed defeat into a school of learning. Ibn Kathir notes particularly the extraordinary behavior of individual Companions at Uhud — Abu Dujana, who used his body as a shield for the Prophet ﷺ; Anas ibn al-Nadr, who entered the thick of battle saying 'Ah, I smell the fragrance of Paradise' and was found with seventy wounds after the battle; and the women of Medina who came out to nurse the wounded and carry water.

The Treaty of Hudaybiyyah and the subsequent Conquest of Mecca are presented by Ibn Kathir in full detail, with particular attention to the Quranic characterization of the treaty as 'a clear victory' — a divine perspective that the subsequent events fully vindicated. The Conquest of Mecca is treated as the fulfillment of the entire prophetic mission in its temporal dimension: the city that had rejected, persecuted, and exiled the Prophet ﷺ was now opened to him in the most complete and nonviolent way, and the mercy shown to its population — the general amnesty that the Prophet ﷺ declared — is presented as one of the greatest demonstrations of prophetic character in human history.

Ibn Kathir concludes the Madinan Seerah with reflections on the magnitude of the transformation achieved: a fractured, polytheistic, tribal society had been unified in faith, organized under divine law, and launched on a trajectory that would carry Islamic civilization across three continents within decades of the Prophet's ﷺ death. This achievement, accomplished by one man and a relatively small community within twenty-three years, is presented as the supreme historical demonstration of the truth of the prophetic mission.
